title: Introduction to mctq
text: mctq makes use lubridate hms packages tidyverse, provide special objects deal date/time values R. dataset conform structure, first need convert data . Due circular nature time, strongly recommend use appropriate temporal objects dealing date/time R. can help get rid several computation mistakes trying adapt data base 10 system rooted base 12 numerical system. Teaching parse/convert data outside scope article. Please refer lubridate hms package documentation learn . two essential packages deal date/time data R. also recommend read Dates times chapter Wickham & Grolemund’s book “R Data Science”. interested two types time objects: Duration objects, store time spans, sleep latency, hms objects, store local time values, bedtime. first, let’s take look data, shall ? ’re using RStudio, can run code type View(data) console explore . can see, data came different formats. example, column bt_w (local time going bed workdays) hours:minutes format, slat_w (sleep latency workdays) duration expressed minutes. fictional data parsed/converted code . Please note lubridate hms packages equipped easy tools great documentation help task. data now set start. Let’s take look .

title: Introduction to mctq
text: mctq provides complete consistent toolkit process Munich ChronoType Questionnaire (MCTQ) data. start process, must first compute MCTQ variables related section questionnaire. ’re going use direct assigning computing MCTQ variables, just straightforward examples. , recommend assigning variables dataset using mutate() function, included dplyr package.

what: fd(): Number of work-free days per week
title: Introduction to mctq
text: fd() simple function allows compute difference number days week (7) number workdays per week (wd). takes wd argument. output must total free days respondent week.
code: data$fd <- fd(data$wd)  # Comparing the result data %>% dplyr::select(wd, fd) #> # A tibble: 5 × 2 #>      wd    fd #>   <int> <int> #> 1     5     2 #> 2     2     5 #> 3     5     2 #> 4     2     5 #> 5     7     0

what: so(): Local time of sleep onset
title: Introduction to mctq
text: () allows compute local time sleep onset workdays (so_w) work-free days (so_f). takes two arguments: sprep (local time preparing sleep) slat (sleep latency time fall asleep preparing sleep). output must sum sprep slat circular time frame 24 hours. circular time frame 24 hours? Run ?cycle_time console click link detail explanation.
code: data$so_w <- so(data$sprep_w, data$slat_w) data$so_f <- so(data$sprep_f, data$slat_f)  # Comparing the result data %>% dplyr::select(sprep_w, slat_w, so_w, sprep_f, slat_f, so_f) #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>   sprep_w slat_w              so_w   sprep_f slat_f              so_f   #>   <time>  <Duration>          <time> <time>  <Duration>          <time> #> 1 23:20   1800s (~30 minutes) 23:50  00:15   300s (~5 minutes)   00:20  #> 2 21:30   300s (~5 minutes)   21:35  23:20   600s (~10 minutes)  23:30  #> 3 23:10   3000s (~50 minutes) 00:00  00:15   1500s (~25 minutes) 00:40  #> 4 01:10   900s (~15 minutes)  01:25  00:55   1500s (~25 minutes) 01:20  #> 5 22:40   300s (~5 minutes)   22:45  00:30   300s (~5 minutes)   00:35

what: gu(): Local time of getting out of bed
title: Introduction to mctq
text: gu() allows compute local time getting bed workdays (gu_w) work-free days (gu_f). takes two arguments: se (local time sleep end) si (sleep inertia). output must sum se si circular time frame 24 hours. Please note , despite name, si represents time respondent takes get sleep end. decided maintain original names abbreviations proposed MCTQ authors.
code: data$gu_w <- gu(data$se_w, data$si_w) data$gu_f <- gu(data$se_f, data$si_f)  # Comparing the result data %>% dplyr::select(se_w, si_w, gu_w, se_f, si_f, gu_f) #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>   se_w   si_w                gu_w   se_f   si_f                gu_f   #>   <time> <Duration>          <time> <time> <Duration>          <time> #> 1 06:55  1500s (~25 minutes) 07:20  10:10  1500s (~25 minutes) 10:35  #> 2 05:05  300s (~5 minutes)   05:10  07:20  1200s (~20 minutes) 07:40  #> 3 08:00  900s (~15 minutes)  08:15  10:05  900s (~15 minutes)  10:20  #> 4 08:55  1200s (~20 minutes) 09:15  07:30  1200s (~20 minutes) 07:50  #> 5 08:20  300s (~5 minutes)   08:25  07:55  1500s (~25 minutes) 08:20

title: Introduction to mctq
text: sdu() allows compute sleep duration workdays (sd_w) work-free days (sd_f). takes two arguments: (local time sleep onset) se (local time sleep end). output must difference se circular time frame 24 hours. Please note , although tried preserve original authors’ naming pattern MCTQ functions, name sd provokes dangerous name collision widely used stats::sd (standard deviation) function. ’s named sdu. sdu() msl() exceptions, mctq functions maintain strong naming resemblance original authors’ naming pattern.
code: data$sd_w <- sdu(data$so_w, data$se_w) data$sd_f <- sdu(data$so_f, data$se_f)  # Comparing the result data %>% dplyr::select(so_w, se_w, sd_w, so_f, se_f, sd_f) #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>   so_w   se_w   sd_w                 so_f   se_f   sd_f                 #>   <time> <time> <Duration>           <time> <time> <Duration>           #> 1 23:50  06:55  25500s (~7.08 hours) 00:20  10:10  35400s (~9.83 hours) #> 2 21:35  05:05  27000s (~7.5 hours)  23:30  07:20  28200s (~7.83 hours) #> 3 00:00  08:00  28800s (~8 hours)    00:40  10:05  33900s (~9.42 hours) #> 4 01:25  08:55  27000s (~7.5 hours)  01:20  07:30  22200s (~6.17 hours) #> 5 22:45  08:20  34500s (~9.58 hours) 00:35  07:55  26400s (~7.33 hours)

title: Introduction to mctq
text: tbt() allows compute total time bed workdays (tbt_w) work-free days (tbt_f). takes two arguments: bt (local time going bed) gu (local time getting bed). output must difference gu bt circular time frame 24 hours.
code: data$tbt_w <- tbt(data$bt_w, data$gu_w) data$tbt_f <- tbt(data$bt_f, data$gu_f)  # Comparing the result data %>% dplyr::select(bt_w, gu_w, tbt_w, bt_f, gu_f, tbt_f) #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>   bt_w   gu_w   tbt_w                bt_f   gu_f   tbt_f                 #>   <time> <time> <Duration>           <time> <time> <Duration>            #> 1 22:50  07:20  30600s (~8.5 hours)  23:35  10:35  39600s (~11 hours)    #> 2 21:25  05:10  27900s (~7.75 hours) 21:55  07:40  35100s (~9.75 hours)  #> 3 22:30  08:15  35100s (~9.75 hours) 22:40  10:20  42000s (~11.67 hours) #> 4 00:45  09:15  30600s (~8.5 hours)  23:15  07:50  30900s (~8.58 hours)  #> 5 22:40  08:25  35100s (~9.75 hours) 00:30  08:20  28200s (~7.83 hours)

title: Introduction to mctq
text: msl() allows compute local time mid-sleep workdays (msw) work-free days (msf). takes two arguments: (local time sleep onset) sd (sleep duration). output must sum half sd duration circular time frame 24 hours.
code: data$msw <- msl(data$so_w, data$sd_w) data$msf <- msl(data$so_f, data$sd_f)  # Comparing the result data %>% dplyr::select(so_w, sd_w, msw, so_f, sd_f, msf) #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>   so_w   sd_w                 msw      so_f   sd_f                 msf      #>   <time> <Duration>           <time>   <time> <Duration>           <time>   #> 1 23:50  25500s (~7.08 hours) 03:22:30 00:20  35400s (~9.83 hours) 05:15:00 #> 2 21:35  27000s (~7.5 hours)  01:20:00 23:30  28200s (~7.83 hours) 03:25:00 #> 3 00:00  28800s (~8 hours)    04:00:00 00:40  33900s (~9.42 hours) 05:22:30 #> 4 01:25  27000s (~7.5 hours)  05:10:00 01:20  22200s (~6.17 hours) 04:25:00 #> 5 22:45  34500s (~9.58 hours) 03:32:30 00:35  26400s (~7.33 hours) 04:15:00

title: Introduction to mctq
text: sd_week() allows compute average weekly sleep duration. takes three arguments: sd_w (sleep duration workdays), sd_f (sleep duration work-free days), wd (number workdays per week). output must weighted mean sd_w sd_f, wd fd(wd) weights, circular time frame 24 hours.
code: data$sd_week <- sd_week(data$sd_w, data$sd_f, data$wd)  # Comparing the result data <- data %>% dplyr::mutate(sd_week_rounded = mctq::round_time(sd_week)) data %>% dplyr::select(wd, sd_w, fd, sd_f, sd_week_rounded) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>      wd sd_w                    fd sd_f                 sd_week_rounded      #>   <int> <Duration>           <int> <Duration>           <Duration>           #> 1     5 25500s (~7.08 hours)     2 35400s (~9.83 hours) 28329s (~7.87 hours) #> 2     2 27000s (~7.5 hours)      5 28200s (~7.83 hours) 27857s (~7.74 hours) #> 3     5 28800s (~8 hours)        2 33900s (~9.42 hours) 30257s (~8.4 hours)  #> 4     2 27000s (~7.5 hours)      5 22200s (~6.17 hours) 23571s (~6.55 hours) #> 5     7 34500s (~9.58 hours)     0 26400s (~7.33 hours) 34500s (~9.58 hours)

title: Introduction to mctq
text: sloss_week() allows compute weekly sleep loss. takes three arguments: sd_w (sleep duration workdays), sd_f (sleep duration work-free days), wd (number workdays per week). sd_week(average weekly sleep duration) greater sd_w, output must difference sd_week sd_w times wd. Else, must return difference sd_week sd_f times fd(wd) (number free days per week). See ?sloss_week learn .
code: data$sloss_week <- sloss_week(data$sd_w, data$sd_f, data$wd)  # Comparing the result data <- data %>% dplyr::mutate(   sloss_week_rounded = mctq::round_time(sloss_week)) data %>% dplyr::select(wd, sd_w, fd, sd_f, sloss_week_rounded) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>      wd sd_w                    fd sd_f                 sloss_week_rounded     #>   <int> <Duration>           <int> <Duration>           <Duration>             #> 1     5 25500s (~7.08 hours)     2 35400s (~9.83 hours) 14143s (~3.93 hours)   #> 2     2 27000s (~7.5 hours)      5 28200s (~7.83 hours) 1714s (~28.57 minutes) #> 3     5 28800s (~8 hours)        2 33900s (~9.42 hours) 7286s (~2.02 hours)    #> 4     2 27000s (~7.5 hours)      5 22200s (~6.17 hours) 6857s (~1.9 hours)     #> 5     7 34500s (~9.58 hours)     0 26400s (~7.33 hours) 0s

title: Introduction to mctq
text: le_week() allows compute average weekly light exposure. takes three arguments: le_w (light exposure workdays), le_f (light exposure work-free days), wd (number workdays per week). output must weighted mean le_w le_f, wd fd(wd) weights, circular time frame 24 hours. what: msf_sc(): Chronotype or sleep-corrected local time of mid-sleep on work-free days
title: Introduction to mctq
text: msf_sc() allows compute chronotype, corrected local time mid-sleep work-free days. takes five arguments: msf (local time mid-sleep work-free days), sd_w (sleep duration workdays), sd_f (sleep duration work-free days), sd_week(average weekly sleep duration), alarm_f (logical object indicating respondent uses alarm clock wake work-free days). sd_f less equal sd_w, output must msf. Else, must return msf minus difference sd_f sd_week divided 2. msf_sc can computed alarm_f equal FALSE (function return NA alarm_f == TRUE). msf_sc applies correction msf, removing estimation effect accumulated sleep debt workdays usually compensated work-free days. See ?msf_sc learn .
code: data$msf_sc <- msf_sc(data$msf, data$sd_w, data$sd_f, data$sd_week,                        data$alarm_f)  # Comparing the result data <- data %>% dplyr::mutate(msf_sc_rounded = mctq::round_time(msf_sc)) data %>% dplyr::select(msf, msf_sc_rounded) #> # A tibble: 5 × 2 #>   msf      msf_sc_rounded #>   <time>   <time>         #> 1 05:15:00 04:16:04       #> 2 03:25:00 03:22:09       #> 3 05:22:30 04:52:09       #> 4 04:25:00 04:25:00       #> 5 04:15:00 04:15:00

text: sjl_rel() allows compute relative social jetlag. takes least two arguments: msw (local time mid-sleep workdays) msf (local time mid-sleep work-free days). output must difference msf msw circular time frame 24 hours. case don’t know, social jet lag concept developed Wittmann et al. (2006) represents discrepancy social biological time. difference described may seem trivial easy compute, ’s . See vignette("sjl-computation", package = "mctq") learn .
code: data$sjl_rel <- sjl_rel(data$msw, data$msf)  # Comparing the result data %>% dplyr::select(msw, msf, sjl_rel) #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>   msw      msf      sjl_rel               #>   <time>   <time>   <Duration>            #> 1 03:22:30 05:15:00 6750s (~1.88 hours)   #> 2 01:20:00 03:25:00 7500s (~2.08 hours)   #> 3 04:00:00 05:22:30 4950s (~1.38 hours)   #> 4 05:10:00 04:25:00 -2700s (~-45 minutes) #> 5 03:32:30 04:15:00 2550s (~42.5 minutes)

title: Why Duration and not Period?
text: lubridate package offers three types objects storing manipulating time spans: Duration, Period, Interval. understand difference Duration Period objects must first remember timeline always consistent, can irregularities caused , example, leap years, DST (Daylight Saving Time), leap seconds. ’s Period objects differ Duration objects. Duration objects represent time spans exact number seconds. , Duration object 1 hour always represent 1-hour time span, even possible timeline irregularities. Period objects work little bit differently. special type object developed lubridate team represents “human units”, ignoring possible timeline irregularities. say 1 day Period can different time spans looking timeline irregular event. illustrate behavior, take case DST event, starting 2016-03-13 01:00:00 EST. might ask: result NA adding 1 hour Period object? ’s Period objects ignore time irregularities. DST starts 01:00:00 timeline “jumps” 03:00:00, period 02:00:00 02:59:59 doesn’t exist. code: ## Scalar example  le_w <- lubridate::dhours(1.5) le_f <- lubridate::dhours(3.7) wd <- 5 le_week(le_w, le_f, wd) #> [1] "7662.85714285714s (~2.13 hours)" #> [1] "7662.85714285714s (~2.13 hours)" # Expected  le_w <- lubridate::dhours(3) le_f <- lubridate::dhours(1.5) wd <- 6 le_week(le_w, le_f, wd) #> [1] "10028.5714285714s (~2.79 hours)" #> [1] "10028.5714285714s (~2.79 hours)" # Expected  le_w <- lubridate::dhours(5.6) le_f <- lubridate::as.duration(NA) wd <- 3 le_week(le_w, le_f, wd) #> [1] NA #> [1] NA # Expected  ## Vector example  le_w <- c(lubridate::dhours(3), lubridate::dhours(2.45)) le_f <- c(lubridate::dhours(3), lubridate::dhours(3.75)) wd <- c(4, 5) le_week(le_w, le_f, wd) #> [1] "10800s (~3 hours)"               "10157.1428571429s (~2.82 hours)" #> [1] "10800s (~3 hours)" # Expected #> [2] "10157.1428571429s (~2.82 hours)" # Expected  ## Checking second output from vector example  if (requireNamespace("stats", quietly = TRUE)) {     i <- 2     x <- c(le_w[i], le_f[i])     w <- c(wd[i], fd(wd[i]))     lubridate::as.duration(stats::weighted.mean(x, w)) } #> [1] "10157.1428571429s (~2.82 hours)" #> [1] "10157.1428571429s (~2.82 hours)" # Expected  ## Converting the output to `hms`  le_w <- lubridate::dhours(1.25) le_f <- lubridate::dhours(6.23) wd <- 3 le_week(le_w, le_f, wd) #> [1] "14744.5714285714s (~4.1 hours)" #> [1] "14744.5714285714s (~4.1 hours)" # Expected  hms::hms(as.numeric(le_week(le_w, le_f, wd))) #> 04:05:44.571429 #> 04:05:44.571429 # Expected  ## Rounding the output at the seconds level  le_w <- lubridate::dhours(3.4094) le_f <- lubridate::dhours(6.2345) wd <- 2 le_week(le_w, le_f, wd) #> [1] "19538.3828571429s (~5.43 hours)" #> [1] "19538.3828571429s (~5.43 hours)" # Expected  round_time(le_week(le_w, le_f, wd)) #> [1] "19538s (~5.43 hours)" #> [1] "19538s (~5.43 hours)" # Expected

title: Compute MCTQ sleep-corrected local time of mid-sleep on work-free days — msf_sc
text: $$\textrm{} Alarm_{F} = True \; , \; MSF_{sc} = \textrm{Available (NA)}$$ $$\textrm{Else } SD_F \leq SD_W \; , \; MSF_{sc} = MSF$$ $$\textrm{Else } \; , \; MSF_{sc} = MSF - \frac{SD_F - SD_{week}}{2}$$ : \(MSF_{sc}\) = Sleep-corrected local time mid-sleep work-free days. \(Alarm_{F}\) = logical value indicating respondent uses alarm clock wake work-free days. \(MSF\) = Local time mid-sleep work-free days. \(SD_W\) = Sleep duration workdays. \(SD_F\) = Sleep duration work-free days. \(SD_{week}\) = Average weekly sleep duration. * \(W\) = Workdays; \(F\) = Work-free days. Note , since: $$MSF = SO_{F} + \frac{SD_{F}}{2}$$ : \(MSF\) = Local time mid-sleep work-free days. \(SO_{F}\) = Local time sleep onset work-free days. \(SD_{F}\) = Sleep duration work-free days. last condition \(MSF_{sc}\) computation can simplified : $$MSF_{sc} = SO_{F} + \frac{SD_{F}}{2} - \frac{SD_{F} - SD_{week}}{2}$$ $$MSF_{sc} = SO_{F} + \frac{SD_{F}}{2} - \frac{SD_{F}}{2} + \frac{SD_{week}}{2}$$ $$MSF_{sc} = SO_{F} + \frac{SD_{week}}{2}$$

text: $$\textrm{} Alarm_{F}^{M/E/N} = True \; , \; MSF_{sc}^{M/E/N} = \textrm{Available (NA)}$$ $$\textrm{Else } SD_{F}^{M/E/N} \leq SD_{W}^{M/E/N} \; , \; MSF_{sc}^{M/E/N} = MSF^{M/E/N}$$ $$\textrm{Else } \; , \; MSF_{sc}^{M/E/N} = MSF^{M/E/N} - \frac{SD_{F}^{M/E/N} - \emptyset SD^{M/E/N}}{2}$$ : \(MSF_{sc}^{M/E/N}\) = Sleep-corrected local time mid-sleep two free days particular shift. \(Alarm_{F}^{M/E/N}\) = logical value indicating respondent uses alarm clock wake two free days particular shift. \(MSF^{M/E/N}\) = Local time mid-sleep two free days particular shift. \(SD_{W}^{M/E/N}\) = Sleep duration two days particular shift. \(SD_{F}^{M/E/N}\) = Sleep duration two free days particular shift. \(\emptyset SD^{M/E/N}\) = Overall sleep duration particular shift. * \(W\) = Workdays; \(F\) = Work-free days, \(M\) = Morning shift; \(E\) = Evening shift; \(N\) = Night shift. Note , since: $$MSF^{M/E/N} = SO_{F}^{M/E/N} + \frac{SD_{F}^{M/E/N}}{2}$$ : \(MSF^{M/E/N}\) = Local time mid-sleep two free days particular shift. \(SO_{F}^{M/E/N}\) = Local time sleep onset two free days particular shift. \(SD_{F}^{M/E/N}\) = Sleep duration two free days particular shift. last condition \(MSF_{sc}^{M/E/N}\) computation can simplified : $$MSF_{sc}^{M/E/N} = SO_{F}^{M/E/N} + \frac{SD_{F}^{M/E/N}}{2} - \frac{SD_{F}^{M/E/N} - \emptyset SD^{M/E/N}}{2}$$ $$MSF_{sc}^{M/E/N} = SO_{F}^{M/E/N} + \frac{SD_{F}^{M/E/N}}{2} - \frac{SD_{F}^{M/E/N}}{2} + \frac{\emptyset SD^{M/E/N}}{2}$$ $$MSF_{sc}^{M/E/N} = SO_{F}^{M/E/N} + \frac{\emptyset SD^{M/E/N}}{2}$$

title: Compute MCTQ sleep-corrected local time of mid-sleep on work-free days — msf_sc
code: ## Scalar example  msf <- hms::parse_hms("04:00:00") sd_w <- lubridate::dhours(6) sd_f <- lubridate::dhours(7) sd_week <- lubridate::dhours(6.29) alarm_f <- FALSE msf_sc(msf, sd_w, sd_f, sd_week, alarm_f) #> 03:38:42 #> 03:38:42 # Expected  msf <- hms::parse_hm("01:00:00") sd_w <- lubridate::dhours(5.5) sd_f <- lubridate::dhours(9) sd_week <- lubridate::dhours(6.75) alarm_f <- FALSE msf_sc(msf, sd_w, sd_f, sd_week, alarm_f) #> 23:52:30 #> 23:52:30 # Expected  msf <- hms::parse_hms("05:40:00") sd_w <- lubridate::dhours(7.5) sd_f <- lubridate::dhours(10) sd_week <- lubridate::dhours(8.5) alarm_f <- TRUE msf_sc(msf, sd_w, sd_f, sd_week, alarm_f) #> NA #> NA # Expected (`msf_sc` cannot be computed if `alarm_f == TRUE`)  ## Vector example  msf <- c(hms::parse_hms("03:45:00"), hms::parse_hm("04:45:00")) sd_w <- c(lubridate::dhours(9), lubridate::dhours(6.45)) sd_f <- c(lubridate::dhours(5), lubridate::dhours(10)) sd_week <- c(lubridate::dhours(8.5), lubridate::dhours(9.2)) alarm_f <- c(FALSE, FALSE) msf_sc(msf, sd_w, sd_f, sd_week, alarm_f) #> 03:45:00 #> 04:21:00 #> 03:45:00 # Expected #> 04:21:00 # Expected  ## Rounding the output at the seconds level  msf <- hms::parse_hms("05:40:00") sd_w <- lubridate::dhours(5.43678) sd_f <- lubridate::dhours(9.345111) sd_week <- lubridate::dhours(7.5453) alarm_f <- FALSE msf_sc(msf, sd_w, sd_f, sd_week, alarm_f) #> 04:46:00.3402 #> 04:46:00.3402 # Expected  round_time(msf_sc(msf, sd_w, sd_f, sd_week, alarm_f)) #> 04:46:00 #> 04:46:00 # Expected

code: ## Scalar example  sd_w <- lubridate::dhours(5) sd_f <- lubridate::dhours(9) n_w <- 2 n_f <- 2 sd_overall(sd_w, sd_f, n_w, n_f) #> [1] "25200s (~7 hours)" #> [1] "25200s (~7 hours)" # Expected  sd_w <- lubridate::dhours(3.45) sd_f <- lubridate::dhours(10) n_w <- 3 n_f <- 1 sd_overall(sd_w, sd_f, n_w, n_f) #> [1] "18315s (~5.09 hours)" #> [1] "18315s (~5.09 hours)" # Expected  sd_w <- lubridate::as.duration(NA) sd_f <- lubridate::dhours(12) n_w <- 4 n_f <- 4 sd_overall(sd_w, sd_f, n_w, n_f) #> [1] NA #> [1] NA # Expected  ## Vector example  sd_w <- c(lubridate::dhours(4), lubridate::dhours(7)) sd_f <- c(lubridate::dhours(12), lubridate::dhours(9)) n_w <- c(3, 4) n_f <- c(2, 4) sd_overall(sd_w, sd_f, n_w, n_f) #> [1] "25920s (~7.2 hours)" "28800s (~8 hours)"   #> [1] "25920s (~7.2 hours)" "28800s (~8 hours)"  # Expected  ## Checking second output from vector example  if (requireNamespace("stats", quietly = TRUE)) {     i <- 2     x <- c(sd_w[i], sd_f[i])     w <- c(n_w[i], n_f[i])     lubridate::as.duration(stats::weighted.mean(x, w)) } #> [1] "28800s (~8 hours)" #> [1] "28800s (~8 hours)" # Expected  ## Converting the output to 'hms'  sd_w <- lubridate::dhours(4.75) sd_f <- lubridate::dhours(10) n_w <- 5 n_f <- 2 sd_overall(sd_w, sd_f, n_w, n_f) #> [1] "22500s (~6.25 hours)" #> [1] "22500s (~6.25 hours)" # Expected  hms::as_hms(as.numeric(sd_overall(sd_w, sd_f, n_w, n_f))) #> 06:15:00 #> 06:15:00 # Expected  ## Rounding the output at the seconds level  sd_w <- lubridate::dhours(5.9874) sd_f <- lubridate::dhours(9.3) n_w <- 3 n_f <- 2 sd_overall(sd_w, sd_f, n_w, n_f) #> [1] "26324.784s (~7.31 hours)" #> [1] "26324.784s (~7.31 hours)" # Expected  round_time(sd_overall(sd_w, sd_f, n_w, n_f)) #> [1] "26325s (~7.31 hours)" #> [1] "26325s (~7.31 hours)" # Expected

what: For standard and micro versions of the MCTQ
title: Compute MCTQ social jetlag — sjl
text: $$SJL_{rel} = MSF - MSW$$ $$SJL = | MSF - MSW |$$ : \(SJL_{rel}\) = Relative social jetlag. \(SJL\) = Absolute social jetlag. \(MSW\) = Local time mid-sleep workdays. \(MSF\) = Local time mid-sleep work-free days. * \(W\) = Workdays; \(F\) = Work-free days.

what: For the shift version of the MCTQ
title: Compute MCTQ social jetlag — sjl
text: $$SJL_{rel}^{M/E/N} = MSF^{M/E/N} - MSW^{M/E/N}$$ $$SJL^{M/E/N} = | MSF^{M/E/N} - MSW^{M/E/N} |$$ : \(SJL_{rel}^{M/E/N}\) = Relative social jetlag particular shift. \(SJL^{M/E/N}\) = Absolute social jetlag particular shift. \(MSW^{M/E/N}\) = Local time mid-sleep two days particular shift. \(MSF^{M/E/N}\) = Local time mid-sleep two free days particular shift. * \(W\) = Workdays; \(F\) = Work-free days, \(M\) = Morning shift; \(E\) = Evening shift; \(N\) = Night shift.

what: Methods for computing the social jetlag
title: Compute MCTQ social jetlag — sjl
text: different approaches compute social jetlag (\(SJL\)). default, sjl() uses approach call "shorter interval approach" ("shorter"). topics provide simple explanation method supported sjl(). get detail understating methods, see vignette("sjl-computation", package = "mctq"). "difference" using method = "difference", sjl() exact computation proposed MCTQ authors, .e., \(SJL\) computed linear difference \(MSF\) \(MSW\) (see Guidelines section). recommend using method, many limitations. "shorter" default method sjl(). based shorter interval \(MSW\) \(MSF\), solving issues relating \(SJL\) computation. "longer" "longer" method uses logic "shorter" method, , instead using shorter interval \(MSW\) \(MSF\), uses longer interval two, considering two-day window. method may help special contexts, like dealing shift-workers greater 12 hours distance mid-sleep hours.

title: Compute MCTQ social jetlag — sjl
code: ## Scalar example  msw <- hms::parse_hm("03:30") msf <- hms::parse_hm("05:00")  sjl(msw, msf) #> [1] "5400s (~1.5 hours)" #> [1] "5400s (~1.5 hours)" # Expected sjl(msw, msf, abs = FALSE) #> [1] "5400s (~1.5 hours)" #> [1] "5400s (~1.5 hours)" # Expected sjl_rel(msw, msf) # Wrapper function #> [1] "5400s (~1.5 hours)" #> [1] "5400s (~1.5 hours)" # Expected  msw <- hms::parse_hm("04:30") msf <- hms::parse_hm("23:30")  sjl(msw, msf) #> [1] "18000s (~5 hours)" #> [1] "18000s (~5 hours)" # Expected sjl(msw, msf, abs = FALSE) #> [1] "-18000s (~-5 hours)" #> [1] "18000s (~-5 hours)" # Expected sjl_rel(msw, msf) # Wrapper function #> [1] "-18000s (~-5 hours)" #> [1] "18000s (~-5 hours)" # Expected  msw <- hms::as_hms(NA) msf <- hms::parse_hm("05:15")  sjl(msw, msf) #> [1] NA #> [1] NA # Expected  ## Vector example  msw <- c(hms::parse_hm("02:05"), hms::parse_hm("04:05")) msf <- c(hms::parse_hm("23:05"), hms::parse_hm("04:05"))  sjl(msw, msf) #> [1] "10800s (~3 hours)" "0s"                #> [1] "10800s (~3 hours)" "0s" # Expected sjl(msw, msf, abs = FALSE) #> [1] "-10800s (~-3 hours)" "0s"                  #> [1] "-10800s (~-3 hours)" "0s" # Expected sjl_rel(msw, msf) # Wrapper function #> [1] "-10800s (~-3 hours)" "0s"                  #> [1] "-10800s (~-3 hours)" "0s" # Expected  ## Using different methods  msw <- hms::parse_hm("19:15") msf <- hms::parse_hm("02:30")  sjl(msw, msf, abs = FALSE, method = "difference") #> [1] "-60300s (~-16.75 hours)" #> [1] "-60300s (~-16.75 hours)" # Expected sjl(msw, msf, abs = FALSE, method = "shorter") # Default method #> [1] "26100s (~7.25 hours)" #> [1] "26100s (~7.25 hours)" # Expected sjl(msw, msf, abs = FALSE, method = "longer") #> [1] "-60300s (~-16.75 hours)" #> [1] "-60300s (~-16.75 hours)" # Expected  msw <- hms::parse_hm("02:45") msf <- hms::parse_hm("04:15")  sjl(msw, msf, abs = FALSE, method = "difference") #> [1] "5400s (~1.5 hours)" #> [1] "5400s (~1.5 hours)" # Expected sjl(msw, msf, abs = FALSE, method = "shorter") # Default method #> [1] "5400s (~1.5 hours)" #> [1] "5400s (~1.5 hours)" # Expected sjl(msw, msf, abs = FALSE, method = "longer") #> [1] "-81000s (~-22.5 hours)" #> [1] "-81000s (~-22.5 hours)" # Expected  ## Converting the output to 'hms'  msw <- hms::parse_hm("01:15") msf <- hms::parse_hm("03:25") sjl(msw, msf) #> [1] "7800s (~2.17 hours)" #> [1] "7800s (~2.17 hours)" # Expected  hms::as_hms(as.numeric(sjl(msw, msf))) #> 02:10:00 #> 02:10:00 # Expected  ## Rounding the output at the seconds level  msw <- hms::parse_hms("04:19:33.1234") msf <- hms::parse_hms("02:55:05") sjl(msw, msf) #> [1] "5068.12339997292s (~1.41 hours)" #> [1] "5068.12339997292s (~1.41 hours)" # Expected  round_time(sjl(msw, msf)) #> [1] "5068s (~1.41 hours)" #> [1] "5068s (~1.41 hours)" # Expected

what: For standard and micro versions of the MCTQ
title: Compute Jankowski's MCTQ sleep-corrected social jetlag — sjl_sc
text: $$\textrm{} SD_{W} > SD_{F} \; \& \; SE_{W} \leq SE_{F} \; , \; SJL_{sc} = | SE_{F} - SE_{W} |$$ $$\textrm{Else } \; , \; SJL_{sc} = | SO_{F} - SO_{W} |$$ : \(SJL_{sc}\) = Jankowski's sleep-corrected social jetlag. \(SO_{W}\) = Local time sleep onset workdays. \(SE_{W}\) = Local time sleep end workdays. \(SO_{F}\) = Local time sleep onset work-free days. \(SE_{F}\) = Local time sleep end work-free days. * \(W\) = Workdays; \(F\) = Work-free days.

what: For the shift version of the MCTQ
title: Compute Jankowski's MCTQ sleep-corrected social jetlag — sjl_sc
text: $$\textrm{} SD_W^{M/E/N} > SD_F^{M/E/N} \; \& \; SE_W^{M/E/N} \leq SE_F^{M/E/N} \; , \; SJL_{sc}^{M/E/N} = | SE_F^{M/E/N} - SE_W^{M/E/N} |$$ $$\textrm{Else } \; , \; | SJL_{sc}^{M/E/N} = SO_F^{M/E/N} - SO_W^{M/E/N} |$$ : \(SJL_{sc}^{M/E/N}\) = Jankowski's sleep-corrected social jetlag particular shift. \(SO_W^{M/E/N}\) = Local time sleep onset two days particular shift. \(SE_W^{M/E/N}\) = Local time sleep end two days particular shift. \(SO_F^{M/E/N}\) = Local time sleep onset two free days particular shift. \(SE_F^{M/E/N}\) = Local time sleep end two free days particular shift. * \(W\) = Workdays; \(F\) = Work-free days, \(M\) = Morning shift; \(E\) = Evening shift; \(N\) = Night shift.

"difference" using method = "difference", sjl_sc() exact computation proposed Jankowski, .e., \(SJL_{sc}\) computed linear difference \(SO_f\)/\(SE_f\) \(SO_W\)/\(SE_W\) (see Guidelines section). recommend using method, many limitations. "shorter" default method sjl_sc(). based shorter interval \(SO_f\)/\(SE_f\) \(SO_W\)/\(SE_W\), solving issues relating \(SJL_{sc}\) computation. "longer" "longer" method uses logic "shorter" method, , instead using shorter interval \(SO_f\)/\(SE_f\) \(SO_W\)/\(SE_W\), uses longer interval two, considering two-day window. method may help special contexts, like dealing shift-workers greater 12 hours distance sleep hours.

code: ## Scalar example  so_w <- hms::parse_hm("02:00") se_w <- hms::parse_hm("10:00") so_f <- hms::parse_hm("01:00") se_f <- hms::parse_hm("08:00")  sjl_sc(so_w, se_w, so_f, se_f) #> [1] "3600s (~1 hours)" #> [1] "3600s (~1 hours)" # Expected sjl_sc(so_w, se_w, so_f, se_f, abs = FALSE) #> [1] "-3600s (~-1 hours)" #> [1] "-3600s (~-1 hours)" # Expected (negative sjl_sc) sjl_sc_rel(so_w, se_w, so_f, se_f) # Wrapper function #> [1] "-3600s (~-1 hours)" #> [1] "-3600s (~-1 hours)" # Expected (negative sjl_sc) sjl(msl(so_w, sdu(so_w, se_w)), msl(so_f, sdu(so_f, se_f))) #> [1] "5400s (~1.5 hours)" #> [1] "5400s (~1.5 hours)" # Expected  so_w <- hms::parse_hm("22:00") se_w <- hms::parse_hm("06:00") so_f <- hms::parse_hm("01:00") se_f <- hms::parse_hm("06:00") # sd_w > sd_f & se_w <= se_f  sjl_sc(so_w, se_w, so_f, se_f) # sjl_sc = | se_f - se_w | #> [1] "0s" #> [1] "0s" # Expected sjl_sc(so_w, se_w, so_f, se_f, abs = FALSE) #> [1] "0s" #> [1] "0s" # Expected sjl_sc_rel(so_w, se_w, so_f, se_f) # Wrapper function #> [1] "0s" #> [1] "0s" # Expected sjl(msl(so_w, sdu(so_w, se_w)), msl(so_f, sdu(so_f, se_f))) #> [1] "5400s (~1.5 hours)" #> [1] "5400s (~1.5 hours)" # Expected  so_f <- hms::as_hms(NA)  sjl_sc(so_w, se_w, so_f, se_f) #> [1] NA #> [1] NA # Expected  ## Vector example  so_w <- c(hms::parse_hm("00:00"), hms::parse_hm("01:00")) se_w <- c(hms::parse_hm("08:00"), hms::parse_hm("07:00")) so_f <- c(hms::parse_hm("01:00"), hms::parse_hm("01:00")) se_f <- c(hms::parse_hm("09:00"), hms::parse_hm("09:00"))  sjl_sc(so_w, se_w, so_f, se_f) #> [1] "3600s (~1 hours)" "0s"               #> [1] "3600s (~1 hours)" "0s" # Expected sjl_sc(so_w, se_w, so_f, se_f, abs = FALSE) #> [1] "3600s (~1 hours)" "0s"               #> [1] "3600s (~1 hours)" "0s" # Expected sjl_sc_rel(so_w, se_w, so_f, se_f) # Wrapper function #> [1] "3600s (~1 hours)" "0s"               #> [1] "3600s (~1 hours)" "0s" # Expected sjl(msl(so_w, sdu(so_w, se_w)), msl(so_f, sdu(so_f, se_f))) #> [1] "3600s (~1 hours)" "3600s (~1 hours)" #> [1] "3600s (~1 hours)" "3600s (~1 hours)" # Expected  ## See other examples in '?sjl()'

title: Compute MCTQ absolute social jetlag across all shifts — sjl_weighted
text: $$\emptyset SJL_{weighted} = \frac{(| SJL^{M} | \times n_{W}^{M}) + (| SJL^{E} | \times n_{W}^{E}) + (| SJL^{N} | \times n_{W}^{N})}{n_{W}^{M} + n_{W}^{E} + n_{W}^{N}}$$ : \(\emptyset SJL_{weighted}\) = Absolute social jetlag across shifts. \(SJL^{M/E/N}\) = Absolute social jetlag shift. \(n_{W}^{M/E/N}\) = Number days worked shift within shift cycle. * \(W\) = Workdays; \(F\) = Work-free days, \(M\) = Morning shift; \(E\) = Evening shift; \(N\) = Night shift.

title: Compute MCTQ absolute social jetlag across all shifts — sjl_weighted
code: ## Scalar example  sjl <- list(sjl_m = lubridate::dhours(1.25),             sjl_e = lubridate::dhours(0.5),             sjl_n = lubridate::dhours(3)) n_w <- list(n_w_m = 3, n_w_e = 1, n_w_n = 4) sjl_weighted(sjl, n_w) #> [1] "7312.5s (~2.03 hours)" #> [1] "7312.5s (~2.03 hours)" # Expected  sjl <- list(sjl_m = lubridate::dhours(1.25),             sjl_e = lubridate::as.duration(NA),             sjl_n = lubridate::dhours(3)) n_w <- list(n_w_m = 3, n_w_e = 1, n_w_n = 4) sjl_weighted(sjl, n_w) #> [1] NA #> [1] NA # Expected  ## Vector example  sjl <- list(sjl_m = c(lubridate::dhours(2), lubridate::dhours(2.45)),             sjl_e = c(lubridate::dhours(3.21), lubridate::as.duration(NA)),             sjl_n = c(lubridate::dhours(1.2), lubridate::dhours(5.32))) n_w <- list(n_w_m = c(1, 3), n_w_e = c(4, 1), n_w_n = c(3, 3)) sjl_weighted(sjl, n_w) #> [1] "8298s (~2.31 hours)" NA                    #> [1] "8298s (~2.31 hours)" NA # Expected  ## Checking the first output from vector example  if (requireNamespace("stats", quietly = TRUE)) {     i <- 1     x <- c(sjl[["sjl_m"]][i], sjl[["sjl_e"]][i], sjl[["sjl_n"]][i])     w <- c(n_w[["n_w_m"]][i], n_w[["n_w_e"]][i], n_w[["n_w_n"]][i])     lubridate::as.duration(stats::weighted.mean(x, w)) } #> [1] "8298s (~2.31 hours)" #> [1] "8298s (~2.31 hours)" # Expected  ## Converting the output to hms  sjl <- list(sjl_m = lubridate::dhours(0.25),             sjl_e = lubridate::dhours(1.2),             sjl_n = lubridate::dhours(4.32)) n_w <- list(n_w_m = 4, n_w_e = 2, n_w_n = 1)  sjl_weighted(sjl, n_w) #> [1] "3970.28571428571s (~1.1 hours)" #> [1] "3970.28571428571s (~1.1 hours)" # Expected  hms::as_hms(as.numeric(sjl_weighted(sjl, n_w))) #> 01:06:10.285714 #> 01:06:10.285714 # Expected  ## Rounding the output at the seconds level  round_time(sjl_weighted(sjl, n_w)) #> [1] "3970s (~1.1 hours)" #> [1] "3970s (~1.1 hours)" # Expected  round_time(hms::as_hms(as.numeric(sjl_weighted(sjl, n_w)))) #> 01:06:10 #> 01:06:10 # Expected

title: Compute MCTQ weekly sleep loss — sloss_week
text: $$\textrm{} SD_{week} > SD_W \; , \; SLoss_{week} = (SD_{week} - SD_W) \times WD$$ $$\textrm{Else } \; , \; SLoss_{week} = (SD_{week} - SD_F) \times FD$$ : \(SLoss_{week}\): Weekly sleep loss. \(SD_W\) = Sleep duration workdays. \(SD_F\) = Sleep duration work-free days. \(SD_{week}\) = Average weekly sleep duration. \(WD\) = Number workdays per week ("regular work schedule work ___ days per week"). \(FD\) = Number work-free days per week. * \(W\) = Workdays; \(F\) = Work-free days.

title: Sum time objects — sum_time
code: ## Non-vectorized sum in an linear time frame  x <- c(as.POSIXct("2020-01-01 15:00:00"), as.POSIXct("1999-05-04 17:30:00")) y <- lubridate::as.interval(lubridate::dhours(7), as.Date("1970-05-08")) sum_time(x, y) #> [1] "142200s (~1.65 days)" #> [1] "142200s (~1.65 days)" # 39:30:00 # Expected  ## Non-vectorized sum in a circular time frame of 24 hours  x <- c(lubridate::dhours(25), lubridate::dhours(5), lubridate::dminutes(50)) sum_time(x, cycle = lubridate::ddays()) #> [1] "24600s (~6.83 hours)" #> [1] "24600s (~6.83 hours)" # 06:50:00 # Expected  x <- c(hms::parse_hm("00:15"), hms::parse_hm("02:30"), hms::as_hms(NA)) sum_time(x, cycle = lubridate::ddays()) #> [1] NA #> NA # Expected sum_time(x, cycle = lubridate::ddays(), na_rm = TRUE) #> [1] "9900s (~2.75 hours)" #> [1] "9900s (~2.75 hours)" # 02:45:00 # Expected  x <- c(lubridate::dhours(-12), lubridate::dhours(-13)) sum_time(x, cycle = lubridate::ddays(), reverse = FALSE) #> [1] "-3600s (~-1 hours)" #> [1] "-3600s (~-1 hours)" # -01:00:00 # Expected  x <- c(lubridate::dhours(-12), lubridate::dhours(-13)) sum_time(x, cycle = lubridate::ddays(), reverse = TRUE) #> [1] "82800s (~23 hours)" #> [1] "82800s (~23 hours)" # 23:00:00 # Expected  ## Vectorized sum in an linear time frame  x <- c(lubridate::dhours(6), NA) y <- c(hms::parse_hm("23:00"), hms::parse_hm("10:00")) vct_sum_time(x, y) #> [1] "104400s (~1.21 days)" NA                     #> [1] "104400s (~1.21 days)" NA # 29:00:00 NA # Expected vct_sum_time(x, y, na_rm = TRUE) #> [1] "104400s (~1.21 days)" "36000s (~10 hours)"   #> [1] "104400s (~1.21 days)" "36000s (~10 hours)" # Expected  ## Vectorized sum in a circular time frame of 24 hours  x <- c(lubridate::dhours(6), NA) y <- c(hms::parse_hm("23:00"), hms::parse_hm("10:00")) vct_sum_time(x, y, cycle = lubridate::ddays()) #> [1] "18000s (~5 hours)" NA                  #> [1] "18000s (~5 hours)" NA  # Expected vct_sum_time(x, y, cycle = lubridate::ddays(), na_rm = TRUE) #> [1] "18000s (~5 hours)"  "36000s (~10 hours)" #> [1] "18000s (~5 hours)"  "36000s (~10 hours)" # Expected  x <- c(lubridate::dhours(-49), lubridate::dhours(-24)) y <- c(hms::parse_hm("24:00"), - hms::parse_hm("06:00")) vct_sum_time(x, y, cycle = lubridate::ddays(), reverse = FALSE) #> [1] "-3600s (~-1 hours)"  "-21600s (~-6 hours)" #> [1] "-3600s (~-1 hours)"  "-21600s (~-6 hours)" # Expected  x <- c(lubridate::dhours(-49), lubridate::dhours(-24)) y <- c(hms::parse_hm("24:00"), - hms::parse_hm("06:00")) vct_sum_time(x, y, cycle = lubridate::ddays(), reverse = TRUE) #> [1] "82800s (~23 hours)" "64800s (~18 hours)" #> [1] "82800s (~23 hours)" "64800s (~18 hours)" # Expected

what: For standard and micro versions of the MCTQ
title: Compute MCTQ total time in bed — tbt
text: $$TBT_{W/F} = GU_{W/F} - BT_{W/F}$$ : \(TBT_{W/F}\) = Total time bed work work-free days. \(GU_{W/F}\) = Local time getting bed work work-free days. \(BT_{W/F}\) = Local time going bed work work-free days ("go bed ___ o'clock"). * \(W\) = Workdays; \(F\) = Work-free days.

what: For the shift version of the MCTQ
title: Compute MCTQ total time in bed — tbt
text: $$TBT_{W/F}^{M/E/N} = GU_{W/F}^{M/E/N} - BT_{W/F}^{M/E/N}$$ : \(TBT_{W/F}^{M/E/N}\) = Total time bed two days particular shift two free days particular shift. \(GU_{W/F}^{M/E/N}\) = Local time getting bed two days particular shift two free days particular shift. \(BT_{W/F}^{M/E/N}\) = Local time going bed two days particular shift two free days particular shift  ("go bed ___ o'clock"). * \(W\) = Workdays; \(F\) = Work-free days, \(M\) = Morning shift; \(E\) = Evening shift; \(N\) = Night shift.
